title: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment
section: Genetics, Aging, and Frontiers in Medicine
authors: Brian C. Capell, Shelley L. Berger

items.subtopic: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment
items.title: Epigenetic Mechanisms Influence Gene Expression Independently of DNA Sequence
items.content: Epigenetics involves mechanisms that influence gene expression and phenotypes without altering the underlying DNA sequence. These changes can be inherited through cell division, impacting various biological processes.
keyPoints: The term 'epigenetics' was coined by Conrad Waddington in 1942.
Epigenetic mechanisms affect gene expression independently of DNA sequence changes.
These changes can sometimes be inherited through cell division.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment

items.subtopic: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment
items.title: Epigenetics as a Promising Field for New Therapies
items.content: Epigenetics is an interdisciplinary field that intersects with various scientific areas, offering opportunities for new therapeutic developments. It plays critical roles in physiology and disease across multiple biomedical fields.
keyPoints: Epigenetics crosses disciplines such as chemistry, genetics, immunology, cancer, aging, and neuroscience.
The field has seen significant innovation due to advanced technologies for studying the epigenome.
Epigenetic-based therapies have shown success in clinical medicine.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment

items.title: Chromatin Compaction Influences Gene Expression
items.content: The organization of DNA into chromatin, specifically through nucleosome formation and higher-order structures like euchromatin and heterochromatin, plays a critical role in regulating gene expression. Euchromatin is associated with active transcription, while heterochromatin is linked to repression.
keyPoints: Nucleosomes consist of 147 base pairs of DNA wrapped around an octamer of histone proteins (two copies each of H2A, H2B, H3, and H4).
Euchromatin is transcriptionally active; heterochromatin is repressed.
Higher order chromatin architecture contributes to gene regulation.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · THE BIOCHEMICAL BASES OF EPIGENETICS (part 1)

items.title: DNA Methylation and Gene Repression
items.content: Cytosine methylation at CpG dinucleotides, primarily mediated by DNMT enzymes, is a key epigenetic modification associated with gene repression. The TET family of enzymes can oxidize methylated cytosines to facilitate demethylation.
keyPoints: DNMT1 maintains methylation post-replication; DNMT3A and 3B add methyl groups de novo.
Cytosine methylation (5-mC) is linked with gene repression.
TET enzymes oxidize 5-methylcytosine to derivatives that facilitate demethylation.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · THE BIOCHEMICAL BASES OF EPIGENETICS (part 1)

items.title: Histone Modifications and Chromatin Regulation
items.content: Posttranslational modifications of histones, such as acetylation and methylation, serve as signals for chromatin regulation. These modifications influence transcription, replication, DNA repair, and recombination by interacting with specific reader proteins.
keyPoints: At least 60 different covalent chemical modifications exist on histone tails and domains.
Histone acetylation and methylation are the most studied modifications.
Phosphorylation of H2A at Ser139 marks DNA double-strand breaks for repair.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · THE BIOCHEMICAL BASES OF EPIGENETICS (part 1)

items.title: Histone Acetylation and Transcriptional Activation
items.content: Histone acetyltransferases (KATs) attach acetyl groups to lysine residues on histones, increasing negative charge and loosening chromatin structure. This modification facilitates transcription factor binding and creates a novel surface for reader proteins, typically leading to transcriptional activation.
keyPoints: Acetylation of H3K9ac or H3K27ac is associated with transcriptional activation.
Acetylation can be rapidly reversed by histone deacetylases (HDACs), including sirtuins.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · THE BIOCHEMICAL BASES OF EPIGENETICS (part 2)

items.title: Histone Methylation and Gene Regulation
items.content: Histone lysine methyltransferases (KMTs) modify histones by adding methyl groups, which can either activate or repress transcription depending on the specific site. For example, H3K4me3 is associated with activation, while H3K27me3 is linked to repression.
keyPoints: H3K4me3, H3K36me3, and H3K79me3 are associated with transcriptional activation.
H3K27me3 and H3K9me3 are linked to transcriptional repression and DNA silencing.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · THE BIOCHEMICAL BASES OF EPIGENETICS (part 2)

items.title: RNA Methylation in Gene Regulation
items.content: RNA methylation, particularly of mRNAs, influences gene expression by affecting mRNA stability and translation. Enzymes like METTL3 and METTL14 add methyl groups co-transcriptionally, while ALKBH5 and FTO remove them.
keyPoints: RNA methylation can lead to mRNA degradation or facilitate translation.
Drugs targeting RNA methylation enzymes are in clinical trials for various diseases.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · THE BIOCHEMICAL BASES OF EPIGENETICS (part 2)

items.subtopic: TOOLS FOR THE STUDY OF EPIGENETICS
items.title: ChIP-seq and Its Advancements in Epigenetic Research
items.content: Chromatin immunoprecipitation followed by sequencing (ChIP-seq) has revolutionized the study of epigenetics by allowing researchers to map histone modifications, DNA-binding transcription factors, and chromatin-modifying enzymes across the entire genome. This technology has been pivotal in understanding gene expression regulation and cellular phenotypes.
keyPoints: ChIP involves crosslinking proteins to DNA using formaldehyde, followed by fragmentation and analysis of DNA.
ChIP-seq combines ChIP with next-generation sequencing for genome-wide analysis.
Technological advancements like CUT&RUN and CUT&Tag have improved signal-to-noise ratio and reduced sample requirements.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · TOOLS FOR THE STUDY OF EPIGENETICS

items.subtopic: TOOLS FOR THE STUDY OF EPIGENETICS
items.title: Understanding 3D Genome Organization with Hi-C Technologies
items.content: Techniques such as HiC and Hi-ChIP have enabled the study of the three-dimensional architecture of chromatin, revealing how nuclear organization influences gene expression. These methods elucidate enhancer-promoter interactions within topologically associated domains (TADs), which are crucial for transcription regulation.
keyPoints: Hi-C is a high-throughput form of chromosome conformation capture technology.
Hi-ChIP combines Hi-C with ChIP-seq to study DNA-binding proteins in 3D genome organization.
Enhancer-promoter loops within TADs facilitate gene expression by increasing promoter-enhancer connections.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · TOOLS FOR THE STUDY OF EPIGENETICS

items.subtopic: TOOLS FOR THE STUDY OF EPIGENETICS
items.title: CRISPR-Based Epigenome Editing for Therapeutic Applications
items.content: CRISPR-based epigenome editing leverages deactivated Cas proteins fused to epigenetic modifiers, guided by RNA sequences, to target specific genes. This approach allows precise modulation of gene expression through histone acetylation or methylation changes, offering potential therapeutic strategies for diseases like cancer.
keyPoints: CRISPR-based editing uses nuclease-deactivated Cas proteins fused with epigenetic modifiers.
Guide RNAs direct these complexes to specific genomic loci to alter gene expression.
Targeting oncogenes such as MYC using CRISPR for cancer treatment is an active area of research.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · TOOLS FOR THE STUDY OF EPIGENETICS

items.title: Epigenetic Profiling in Tumor Origin Identification
items.content: Epigenome profiling of chromatin landscapes can more accurately determine the tissue origin of tumors than DNA sequencing of gene mutations.
keyPoints: Chromatin profiles display cell-type specificity, especially at gene enhancers.
Epigenetic profiling is a better index for tumor origin determination compared to DNA mutation analysis.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 1)

items.title: Role of Histone Modifications in Cell Fate
items.content: Histone modifications like H3K4me3 and H3K27me3 are crucial for cell fate determination, with bivalent states allowing genes to be poised for activation or repression.
keyPoints: Bivalent state: H3K4me3 (activating) and H3K27me3 (repressive).
H3K9me3 acts as a barrier to reprogramming; inhibiting SUV39H1 enhances efficiency.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 1)

items.title: Epigenetic Regulation in Stem Cell Maintenance and Cancer
items.content: Key epigenetic regulators like DNMT1, BMI1, HDACs, and LSD1 are essential for balancing stem cell self-renewal and differentiation, with dysregulation contributing to cancer.
keyPoints: DNMT1 is necessary for stem cell self-renewal; its levels decrease during epidermal stratification.
BMI1 represses genes like p16 and p19ARF, crucial for preventing premature differentiation.
EZH2 overexpression and KDM6B loss disrupt p16 regulation in cancers like SCC.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 2) | EPIGENETICS OF METABOLISM
items.title: Role of EZH2 and KDM6 in Cell-Type Specificity
items.content: Chromatin-modifying enzymes like EZH2, which modifies H3K27me3, promote adipogenesis while inhibiting osteogenesis. Conversely, demethylases such as KDM6A (UTX) and KDM6B drive stem cells toward osteogenesis by derepressing the same genes.
keyPoints: EZH2 promotes adipogenesis and inhibits osteogenesis via H3K27me3 modification.
KDM6A (UTX) and KDM6B promote osteogenesis by demethylating H3K27me3.
Loss of epigenetic regulation can reduce cell differentiation, influencing diseases like cancer.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 2) | EPIGENETICS OF METABOLISM

items.title: Dietary Influence on Epigenetic Regulation and Metabolic Pathways
items.content: High-fat diets inhibit enzymes ACLY and ACSS2, reducing histone acetylation by lowering acetyl-CoA levels. This affects gene expression related to growth and cell cycle progression, impacting stem cell differentiation and autophagy.
keyPoints: High-fat diets reduce histone acetylation by inhibiting ACLY and ACSS2.
Acetyl-CoA is the best predictor of histone acetylation levels.
Increased acetyl-CoA correlates with rising histone acetylation, affecting cell proliferation.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 2) | EPIGENETICS OF METABOLISM

items.title: Metabolic Enzymes as Epigenetic Regulators in the Nucleus
items.content: Certain metabolic enzymes like ACLY, PDH, and ACSS2 are present in the nucleus, directly regulating histone acetylation. These enzymes generate acetyl-CoA from various substrates, influencing gene expression and physiological responses.
keyPoints: Metabolic enzymes such as ACLY, PDH, and ACSS2 can regulate histone acetylation in the nucleus.
ACCLY generates acetyl-CoA from citrate; PDH from pyruvate; ACSS2 from acetate.
Nuclear presence of these enzymes links metabolism directly to gene transcription.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 2) | EPIGENETICS OF METABOLISM

items.title: Epigenetic Dysregulation's Role in Cancer
items.content: Cancer involves both genetic and epigenetic changes, with epigenetic dysregulation playing a significant role. This includes contributing to chemotherapy resistance and antitumor immunity failure.
keyPoints: Epigenetic mechanisms are pervasive in human cancers.
Chromatin-regulating enzymes are robust targets for novel small-molecule inhibitors.
25–30% of cancer driver mutations occur in chromatin regulatory proteins.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · CANCER EPIGENETICS (part 1)

items.title: EZH2 as an Oncogene
items.content: The H3K27me3 histone methyltransferase EZH2 acts as an oncogene, being overexpressed in advanced-stage or metastatic solid tumors like breast cancer, prostate cancer, and melanoma.
keyPoints: EZH2 represses the p16 tumor suppressor via H3K27me3 deposition.
Inhibitors of EZH2 show therapeutic success in preclinical models for cancers such as B-cell lymphoma and melanoma.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · CANCER EPIGENETICS (part 1)

items.title: Oncohistone Mutations
items.content: Histone mutations, specifically H3.3 K27M in pediatric high-grade gliomas and H3.3 K36M in chondroblastomas, act as dominant negative mutations leading to global defects in histone modifications.
keyPoints: H3.3 K27M and K36M mutations promote resistance to apoptosis and failure of normal differentiation.
>90% of chondroblastomas have the H3.3 K36M mutation.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · CANCER EPIGENETICS (part 1)

items.title: Epigenetic Therapies in Cancer: Mechanisms and Clinical Applications
items.content: Epigenetic therapies target reversible epigenetic changes to treat cancer. DNA methylation inhibitors, such as azacitidine and decitabine, reactivate tumor-suppressor genes by inhibiting DNMT1, leading to global hypomethylation. These are used for myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS) and acute myeloid leukemia (AML). HDAC inhibitors (HDACi), effective in cutaneous T-cell lymphoma and multiple myeloma, address loss of acetylation. BET inhibitors (BETi) block the binding of bromodomain-containing proteins to lysine acetylated target proteins, reducing oncogene expression like MYC.
keyPoints: DNA methylation inhibitors reactivate tumor-suppressor genes by inhibiting DNMT1.
Azacitidine and decitabine are used for MDS and AML.
HDAC inhibitors are approved for cutaneous T-cell lymphoma and multiple myeloma.
BET inhibitors reduce MYC expression in hematologic cancers.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · CANCER EPIGENETICS (part 2)

items.title: Emerging Epigenetic Targeted Therapies: Specific Inhibitors and Combinatorial Approaches
items.content: The development of specific epigenome-targeted inhibitors is advancing, with over 30 compounds in various stages of development. Notable examples include EZH2 inhibitors for lymphomas, sarcomas, and melanoma; IDH inhibitors for AML and gliomas with mutant IDH1 or IDH2 genes; LSD1 inhibitors for AML and small-cell lung cancer; and DOT1L and MLL1 inhibitors for leukemias with activated MLL1. These therapies are being optimized in combination with conventional chemotherapies, immunotherapies, radiation, and hormone therapies to enhance patient responses.
keyPoints: Over 30 specific epigenome-targeted inhibitors are in development or preclinical trials.
EZH2 inhibitors show promise for lymphomas, sarcomas, and melanoma.
IDH inhibitors target AML and gliomas with mutant IDH1/2 genes.
LSD1 inhibitors are used for AML and small-cell lung cancer.
DOT1L and MLL1 inhibitors are effective in leukemias with activated MLL1.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · CANCER EPIGENETICS (part 2)

items.title: Histone Modifications and Aging
items.content: Aging is associated with dysregulation of histone modifications, including a loss of repressive marks like H3K9me3 and H3K27me3. This leads to transcriptional instability and aberrant gene expression.
keyPoints: Histone levels, particularly H3 and H4, can reverse age-related changes in mammalian cells and yeast.
Premature aging diseases such as Hutchinson-Gilford progeria syndrome (HGPS) show reduced levels of repressive chromatin marks like H3K9me3 and H3K27me3.
Aged human cells display loss of H3K9me3, leading to activation of transposable elements linked with neurodegenerative disorders.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF AGING (part 1)

items.title: Impact of Chromatin-Modifying Enzymes on Lifespan
items.content: Manipulating chromatin-modifying enzymes affects lifespan in model organisms. Inhibiting H3K27me3 demethylase extends lifespan, while reducing activating H3K4me3 enzymes also promotes longevity.
keyPoints: Inhibition of KDM6A increases repressive H3K27me3 and extends lifespan in C. elegans.
Genetic reduction of enzymes adding H3K4me3 (ash-2, set-2, wdr-5) also extends lifespan in C. elegans.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF AGING (part 1)

items.title: Role of Histone Acetylation and Sirtuins in Aging
items.content: Histone acetylation dysregulation affects aging, with sirtuin deacetylases playing a crucial role. Caloric restriction influences sirtuin expression, impacting health span and lifespan.
keyPoints: Dysregulation of HATs and HDACs is linked to reduced longevity.
Sirtuins like Sirt1 and Sirt6 are key mediators of caloric restriction's pro-longevity effects.
Loss of Sirt6 leads to premature aging in mice, while increased expression from caloric restriction delays aging.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF AGING (part 1)

items.title: DNA Methylation Changes with Aging
items.content: Aging is associated with global DNA hypomethylation and localized hypermethylation at gene promoters, affecting transcription and cellular identity.
keyPoints: 5-mC levels are reduced in senescent human cells.
Global DNA hypomethylation occurs across the genome with aging.
Local regions of hypermethylation occur near CpGs at gene promoters.
DNA methylation status is a highly accurate predictor of chronologic age.
Methylation patterns outperform p16 expression levels and telomere length as biomarkers of aging.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF AGING (part 2)

items.title: Impact of DNA Methylation on Gene Expression in Aging
items.content: Epigenetic disruptions, particularly DNA hypermethylation at gene promoters, lead to transcriptional dysregulation and affect cellular differentiation.
keyPoints: DNA hypermethylation blocks transcription factor binding in hematopoietic stem cells.
This results in dysregulation of normal gene expression with aging.
Epigenetic changes are not merely stochastic but predict chronological age accurately.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF AGING (part 2)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETICS OF THE BRAIN AND BEHAVIOR
items.title: Role of MECP2 in Rett Syndrome
items.content: Mutations in the methyl CpG binding protein 2 (MECP2) are a major cause of Rett syndrome, leading to overactive gene transcription and impaired presynaptic excitatory functions.
keyPoints: MECP2 mutations lead to Rett syndrome.
Loss of MECP2 results in overactive gene transcription in neurons.
Impaired presynaptic excitatory functions are a consequence.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF THE BRAIN AND BEHAVIOR

items.subtopic: EPIGENETICS OF THE BRAIN AND BEHAVIOR
items.title: Epigenetic Dysregulation in Neurodegenerative Diseases
items.content: Altered methylation dynamics, such as DNA hypomethylation, are implicated in neurodegenerative conditions like Parkinson's disease and Alzheimer's disease.
keyPoints: Parkinson's disease involves hypomethylation of genes including SCNA due to DNMT1 depletion.
In Alzheimer's disease, DNA hypomethylation occurs at promoters of key pathogenic genes such as APP.
APP promoter methylation is responsive to environmental factors like aging.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF THE BRAIN AND BEHAVIOR

items.subtopic: EPIGENETICS OF THE BRAIN AND BEHAVIOR
items.title: Epigenetic Influence on Addiction and Behavioral Disorders
items.content: Disruption of epigenetic homeostasis by addictive substances can lead to changes in chromatin structure, influencing reward-seeking behaviors.
keyPoints: Cocaine exposure is associated with acetylation of regulatory elements in the FOSB gene.
Opioid exposure increases histone acetylation and reduces histone methylation, promoting a hyperresponsive state.
Ethanol induces histone acetylation affecting learning and memory.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF THE BRAIN AND BEHAVIOR

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 1)
items.title: Role of Epigenetics in Immune Response Modulation
items.content: Epigenetic mechanisms regulate immune responses by altering gene expression patterns. HATs and HDACs interact with transcription factors like AP-1 and NF-$\kappa$B to modulate inflammatory genes, influencing conditions such as asthma.
keyPoints: HATs activate while HDACs repress inflammatory genes in response to immune-stimulating agents.
Corticosteroids recruit HDAC2 to prevent activation of NF-$\kappa$B-stimulated genes during asthma treatment.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 1)
items.title: Epigenetic Regulation of Type 1 IFN Responses
items.content: Type 1 IFN responses are controlled by epigenetic mechanisms involving H3K9 methyltransferases and chromatin remodeling complexes. These processes regulate the transcriptional activation of IFN-stimulated genes.
keyPoints: H3K9 methyltransferases G9a (EHMT2) and EHMT1 suppress IFN gene expression in an unstimulated state.
STATs recruit BAF and HATs like p300, CBP, GCN5 for chromatin remodeling upon IFN induction.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 1)
items.title: Epigenetic Modifications in Adaptive Immunity
items.content: Adaptive immunity pathways are influenced by epigenetic changes, particularly histone modifications and DNA methylation. These alterations affect T cell differentiation and function, with implications for autoimmune diseases.
keyPoints: CD4+ and CD8+ T cells show distinct histone modification profiles during differentiation.
Hypermethylation of FOXP3 in CD4+ T cells from RA patients affects regulatory T cell activation.
Hypermethylation of CTLA4 locus impairs immunosuppressive abilities in RA.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 2) | CONCLUSION
items.title: Epigenome-Modifying Drugs in Inflammatory and Immune Conditions
items.content: Epigenome-modifying drugs such as DNA methylation inhibitors (azacitidine and decitabine) and HDAC inhibitors (e.g., vorinostat, panobinostat, trichostatin A) have shown potential in treating inflammatory and immune-related conditions by modulating gene expression. These drugs can suppress or enhance specific immune responses, offering therapeutic benefits across various diseases.
keyPoints: DNA methylation inhibitors like azacitidine and decitabine may exert immunosuppressive effects through enhanced FOXP3 expression.
HDAC inhibitors (e.g., vorinostat, panobinostat) can inhibit cytokine production in macrophages from RA patients and block B-cell responses and antibody production.
Trichostatin A has shown efficacy in treating RA, SLE, asthma, acute kidney injury, sepsis-induced lung/cardiac damage, and acute pancreatitis.
BET inhibitors (BETi) have protective effects in conditions like autoimmunity, sepsis, atherosclerosis, psoriasis, periodontitis, and arthritis.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 2) | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 2) | CONCLUSION
items.title: Specific Epigenetic Inhibitors for Anti-Inflammatory Activity
items.content: GSK-J4 and MLL1 inhibitors represent targeted epigenetic therapies with anti-inflammatory properties. GSK-J4 inhibits H3K27me3 demethylases, preventing the loss of repression over inflammatory genes, while MLL1 inhibition blocks proinflammatory cytokine gene expression.
keyPoints: GSK-J4 is a specific inhibitor of H3K27me3 demethylases KDM6A and KDM6B, showing anti-inflammatory activity by maintaining repression over inflammatory genes.
Inhibition of the H3K4me3 histone methyltransferase MLL1 prevents proinflammatory cytokine gene expression induction.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 2) | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment
items.question: Who coined the term 'epigenetics'?
items.explanation: The term epigenetics was coined by Conrad Waddington in 1942.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment

items.subtopic: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment
items.question: What does the prefix 'epi-' mean in the context of genetics?
items.explanation: 'Epi-' means 'over, outside of, around', aptly describing mechanisms influencing gene expression independent of DNA sequence changes.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment

items.subtopic: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment
items.question: Which field does epigenetics cross virtually all areas of?
options: ['Chemistry and genetics, development and differentiation, immunology, cancer, aging, neuroscience']
items.explanation: Epigenetics crosses virtually all areas including chemistry and genetics, development and differentiation, immunology, cancer, aging, and neuroscience.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment

items.question: Which histone proteins are part of the nucleosome core?
items.explanation: The nucleosome core consists of two copies each of the histone proteins H2A, H2B, H3, and H4.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · THE BIOCHEMICAL BASES OF EPIGENETICS (part 1)

items.question: What is the primary function of cytosine methylation at CpG dinucleotides in DNA?
items.explanation: Cytosine methylation at CpG dinucleotides is associated with gene repression.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · THE BIOCHEMICAL BASES OF EPIGENETICS (part 1)

items.question: Which enzyme is critical for maintaining DNA methylation after replication?
items.explanation: DNMT1 is critical for the maintenance of the methylation state after DNA replication.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · THE BIOCHEMICAL BASES OF EPIGENETICS (part 1)

items.question: What role does histone phosphorylation at Ser139 play?
items.explanation: Histone phosphorylation at Ser139 marks DNA double-strand breaks and is critical for the recruitment of the DNA repair machinery.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · THE BIOCHEMICAL BASES OF EPIGENETICS (part 1)

items.question: Which histone modification is typically associated with transcriptional activation?
items.explanation: Acetylation of histone H3 at lysine 27 (H3K27ac) is typically associated with transcriptional activation.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · THE BIOCHEMICAL BASES OF EPIGENETICS (part 2)

items.question: What is the effect of acetylation on histone tails?
options: It increases positive charge and tightens chromatin structure.
It decreases negative charge and loosens chromatin structure.
It creates a novel binding surface for reader proteins and loosens chromatin structure.
It removes lysine residues from histones.
items.explanation: Acetylation of histone tails results in a novel binding surface for reader proteins and a loosening of the chromatin structure, making it more permissive to transcription factor binding.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · THE BIOCHEMICAL BASES OF EPIGENETICS (part 2)

items.question: Which enzyme class is responsible for removing acetyl groups from histones?
items.explanation: Histone deacetylases (HDACs) are responsible for removing acetyl groups from histones, returning them to their unmodified state.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · THE BIOCHEMICAL BASES OF EPIGENETICS (part 2)

items.question: Which histone modification is associated with DNA repeat and centromeric silencing?
items.explanation: Methylation of histone H3 at lysine 9 (H3K9me3) is associated with DNA repeat and centromeric silencing.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · THE BIOCHEMICAL BASES OF EPIGENETICS (part 2)

items.subtopic: TOOLS FOR THE STUDY OF EPIGENETICS
items.question: Which technique involves using a Tn5 transposase to insert sequencing adapters into open regions of chromatin?
items.explanation: ATAC-seq uses a Tn5 transposase to insert sequencing adapters into open regions of chromatin.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · TOOLS FOR THE STUDY OF EPIGENETICS

items.subtopic: TOOLS FOR THE STUDY OF EPIGENETICS
items.question: What is the primary advantage of CUT&RUN over standard ChIP-seq?
options: It allows for single-cell level analysis.
It reduces the number of cells and DNA required.
It profiles histone modifications at the single-cell level.
items.explanation: CUT&RUN significantly scales down the signal-to-noise ratio and reduces the number of cells and DNA required compared to standard ChIP-seq.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · TOOLS FOR THE STUDY OF EPIGENETICS

items.subtopic: TOOLS FOR THE STUDY OF EPIGENETICS
items.question: Which technique combines Hi-C with ChIP-sequencing?
items.explanation: Hi-ChIP combines Hi-C with ChIP-sequencing to study the relationship of DNA-binding proteins to 3D genome organization.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · TOOLS FOR THE STUDY OF EPIGENETICS

items.subtopic: TOOLS FOR THE STUDY OF EPIGENETICS
items.question: What is a major therapeutic potential of CRISPR-based epigenome editing?
options: It can be used to study chromatin accessibility.
It allows for the capture of molecular data at subcellular resolution.
It enables precise targeting of gene regulatory effectors to modify histone levels.
It profiles DNA-binding transcription factors across the genome.
items.explanation: CRISPR-based epigenome editing allows for precise targeting of gene regulatory effectors to change gene's histone acetylation or methylation levels, offering therapeutic potential.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · TOOLS FOR THE STUDY OF EPIGENETICS

items.question: Which histone modification marks genes in embryonic stem cells as 'poised' for activation or repression?
items.explanation: Genes in embryonic stem cells are marked by both activating (H3K4me3) and repressive (H3K27me3) histone modifications, making them 'poised'.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 1)

items.question: What role does DNA methylation play in development?
options: It is involved only in sex chromosome dosage compensation.
It coordinates expression of imprinted genes and other developmental pathways.
It has no significant role in development.
items.explanation: DNA methylation contributes to various developmental processes, including sex chromosome dosage compensation and coordination of imprinted gene expression.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 1)

items.question: Which enzyme's inhibition can enhance reprogramming efficiency when creating induced pluripotent stem cells?
items.explanation: Inhibition of SUV39H1, which catalyzes H3K9me3, can enhance reprogramming efficiency.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 1)

items.question: What is the consequence of loss of BMI1 in stem cells?
options: Stable repression of alternative cell fates
items.explanation: Loss of BMI1 leads to premature differentiation and defective self-renewal in stem cells.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 2) | EPIGENETICS OF METABOLISM
items.question: Which enzyme complex is required for adipogenesis by interacting with PPARγ?
items.explanation: The gene-activating H3K4 histone methyltransferases, MLL3 (KMT2C) and MLL4, are required for adipogenesis by forming a complex with the transcriptional activator ASC2 and the transcription factor PPARγ.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 2) | EPIGENETICS OF METABOLISM

items.subtopic: EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 2) | EPIGENETICS OF METABOLISM
items.question: What is a direct consequence of high levels of acetyl-CoA in cells?
items.explanation: High levels of acetyl-CoA can delay stem cell differentiation and suppress autophagy.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 2) | EPIGENETICS OF METABOLISM

items.subtopic: EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 2) | EPIGENETICS OF METABOLISM
items.question: Which metabolic enzyme is chromatin-bound to regulate gene expression?
items.explanation: ACSS2 can be chromatin-bound to regulate gene expression, leading to physiological responses such as autophagy in the liver and mammalian hippocampal function in learning.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 2) | EPIGENETICS OF METABOLISM

items.subtopic: EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 2) | EPIGENETICS OF METABOLISM
items.question: What is a consequence of mutations in IDH1/2, FH, and SDH genes?
items.explanation: Cancers with mutations in metabolic regulatory genes such as IDH1/2, FH, and SDH lead to the accumulation of by-products that inhibit α-KG-dependent histone demethylases.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 2) | EPIGENETICS OF METABOLISM

items.question: Which chromatin regulatory protein is frequently mutated and acts as a tumor suppressor across various tissues?
items.explanation: MLL3 is mentioned as having tumor-suppressive activity.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · CANCER EPIGENETICS (part 1)

items.question: What type of mutation in histone H3.3 is commonly found in pediatric high-grade gliomas?
items.explanation: The passage states that K27M mutations are common in pediatric high-grade gliomas.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · CANCER EPIGENETICS (part 1)

items.question: Which enzyme is overexpressed in many advanced-stage or metastatic solid tumors and acts as an oncogene?
items.explanation: EZH2 is identified as an oncogene that is overexpressed in many advanced-stage or metastatic solid tumors.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · CANCER EPIGENETICS (part 1)

items.question: What percentage of cancer driver mutations occur in chromatin regulatory proteins according to The Cancer Genome Atlas consortium?
items.explanation: The passage notes that 25–30% of identified cancer driver mutations occur in chromatin regulatory proteins.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · CANCER EPIGENETICS (part 1)

items.question: Which epigenetic therapeutic is used to reactivate tumor-suppressor genes?
items.explanation: The first epigenetic therapeutic involved the use of DNA methylation inhibitors (DNMTi) to reactivate tumor-suppressor genes.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · CANCER EPIGENETICS (part 2)

items.question: Which class of drugs is approved for treatment in cutaneous T-cell lymphoma and multiple myeloma?
items.explanation: HDAC inhibitors (HDACi) are effective and approved for treatment in cutaneous T-cell lymphoma and multiple myeloma.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · CANCER EPIGENETICS (part 2)

items.question: What is the primary mechanism of action of BET inhibitors?
options: Block binding to lysine acetylated proteins
items.explanation: BET inhibitors (BETi) block the binding of Bromodomain-containing proteins to lysine acetylated target proteins.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · CANCER EPIGENETICS (part 2)

items.question: Which histone proteins have been shown to reverse age-related changes when experimentally increased in mammalian cells and yeast?
items.explanation: Experimental increases in histone levels, particularly histones H3 and H4, can reverse age-related changes.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF AGING (part 1)

items.question: What is the effect of inhibiting the H3K27me3 histone demethylase KDM6A in Caenorhabditis elegans?
options: Increased repressive H3K27me3 and extended lifespan
items.explanation: Inhibiting KDM6A results in increased repressive H3K27me3 and extended lifespan.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF AGING (part 1)

items.question: What is a common consequence of cellular senescence regarding chromatin structure?
options: Destabilization of compact heterochromatin adjacent to the nuclear periphery
No change in euchromatic histone modification
items.explanation: Cellular senescence leads to destabilization of compact heterochromatin adjacent to the nuclear periphery.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF AGING (part 1)

items.question: Which sirtuin deacetylase is associated with promoting health span and lifespan across species?
items.explanation: Sirt1 and Sirt6 are key mediators of pro-longevity effects, promoting health span and lifespan.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF AGING (part 1)

items.question: Which epigenetic alteration is associated with a reduction in levels of 5-mC in senescent human cells?
items.explanation: Levels of 5-mC are reduced due to DNA methylation changes in senescent human cells.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF AGING (part 2)

items.question: What is the effect of local regions of hypermethylation near CpGs at gene promoters during aging?
options: Blocks proper binding of transcription factors
Has no impact on gene expression
items.explanation: Local hypermethylation near CpGs at gene promoters blocks the proper binding of transcription factors.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF AGING (part 2)

items.question: What is a highly accurate predictor of chronologic age in human tissues?
options: DNA methylation status at specific CpG sites
items.explanation: The methylation status of a defined number of CpG sites is a highly accurate predictor of chronologic age.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF AGING (part 2)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETICS OF THE BRAIN AND BEHAVIOR
items.question: Which gene mutation is the major cause of Rett syndrome?
items.explanation: Mutations in methyl CpG binding protein 2 (MECP2) are the major cause of Rett syndrome.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF THE BRAIN AND BEHAVIOR

items.subtopic: EPIGENETICS OF THE BRAIN AND BEHAVIOR
items.question: What is a common epigenetic feature observed in Alzheimer's disease?
items.explanation: In Alzheimer's disease, there is significant enrichment of H3K9 and H3K27 acetylation.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF THE BRAIN AND BEHAVIOR

items.subtopic: EPIGENETICS OF THE BRAIN AND BEHAVIOR
items.question: Which syndrome is associated with mutations in the H3K4me1 histone methyltransferase, MLL4?
items.explanation: Kabuki syndrome is caused by germline mutations in either the H3K4me1 histone methyltransferase, MLL4 (KMT2D), or UTX.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF THE BRAIN AND BEHAVIOR

items.subtopic: EPIGENETICS OF THE BRAIN AND BEHAVIOR
items.question: Which neurodegenerative disease involves DNA hypomethylation and decreased histone acetylation due to altered function of CBP?
items.explanation: In Huntington's disease, DNA hypomethylation and decreased histone acetylation are due to altered function of the acetyl transferase CBP.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF THE BRAIN AND BEHAVIOR

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 1)
items.question: Which enzymes are recruited by corticosteroids to prevent the activation of NF-κB-stimulated inflammatory genes during asthma treatment?
items.explanation: Corticosteroids recruit HDAC2 to promoters of NF-κB-stimulated inflammatory genes.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 1)
items.question: What is the role of H3K9 methyltransferases G9a and EHMT1 in unstimulated cells?
options: They suppress expression of IFN and IFN-induced genes.
items.explanation: In an unstimulated state, G9a and EHMT1 suppress expression of IFN and IFN-induced genes.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 1)
items.question: Which histone modification is enriched in effector T-cell function genes of CD8+ memory T cells compared to naive T cells?
items.explanation: Genes associated with effector T-cell functions in CD8+ memory T cells display enrichment of H3K4me3.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 1)
items.question: What is the impact of hypermethylation at the FOXP3 gene in CD4+ T cells from individuals with rheumatoid arthritis?
options: It activates regulatory T cells that dampen immune responses.
It impairs immunosuppressive abilities of regulatory T cells.
It promotes expression of inflammatory genes.
It enhances the recruitment of HATs.
items.explanation: Hypermethylation at the FOXP3 gene activates regulatory T cells that dampen immune responses.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 2) | CONCLUSION
items.question: Which of the following drugs is a DNA methylation inhibitor with immunosuppressive effects mediated by enhanced expression of FOXP3?
items.explanation: Azacitidine and decitabine are DNA methylation inhibitors with immunosuppressive effects mediated by enhanced expression of FOXP3.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 2) | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 2) | CONCLUSION
items.question: Which HDAC inhibitor has shown efficacy in treating RA, SLE, asthma, and other conditions?
items.explanation: Trichostatin A (TSA) has shown efficacy in treating RA, SLE, asthma, and other conditions.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 2) | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 2) | CONCLUSION
items.question: What is the presumed mechanism of anti-inflammatory activity for GSK-J4?
options: Inhibition of H3K27me3 demethylases KDM6A and KDM6B
items.explanation: GSK-J4's anti-inflammatory activity is presumed to be due to inhibition of the H3K27me3 demethylases KDM6A and KDM6B.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 2) | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment
items.statement: Epigenetic changes can be inherited through cell division.
items.explanation: Gene expression and phenotypes influenced by epigenetics can sometimes be inherited through cell division.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment

items.subtopic: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment
items.statement: Epigenetics only involves changes to the DNA sequence.
items.explanation: Epigenetics involves mechanisms that influence gene expression independent of any changes to the underlying DNA sequence.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment

items.statement: Euchromatin regions are typically transcriptionally repressed.
items.explanation: Euchromatin is generally associated with transcriptional activity.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · THE BIOCHEMICAL BASES OF EPIGENETICS (part 1)

items.statement: Polyubiquitination tags proteins for degradation by the proteasome.
items.explanation: Polyubiquitination serves to tag proteins for degradation by the proteasome.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · THE BIOCHEMICAL BASES OF EPIGENETICS (part 1)

items.statement: Lysine methylation alters the electrostatic charge of histone tails.
items.explanation: Lysine methylation does not alter the electrostatic charge of histone tails; it provides more nuanced regulation through effector protein binding.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · THE BIOCHEMICAL BASES OF EPIGENETICS (part 2)

items.statement: RNA methylation can occur co-transcriptionally.
items.explanation: mRNA methylation occurs co-transcriptionally, impacting mRNA degradation or facilitating translation.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · THE BIOCHEMICAL BASES OF EPIGENETICS (part 2)

items.subtopic: TOOLS FOR THE STUDY OF EPIGENETICS
items.statement: ChIP-seq allows for the study of three-dimensional genome organization.
items.explanation: ChIP-seq does not provide information on the physical organization and folding of the genome; this is studied by techniques like Hi-C.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · TOOLS FOR THE STUDY OF EPIGENETICS

items.subtopic: TOOLS FOR THE STUDY OF EPIGENETICS
items.statement: CUT&Tag allows for profiling histone modifications at the single-cell level.
items.explanation: CUT&Tag, a modification of CUT&RUN, enables profiling histone modifications at the single-cell level.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · TOOLS FOR THE STUDY OF EPIGENETICS

items.statement: Epigenome profiling is more effective than DNA sequencing for identifying the tissue of origin in tumors.
items.explanation: Epigenome profiling provides a better index of the origin tissue in tumors compared to DNA sequencing.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 1)

items.statement: H3K9me3 serves as an activating mark for cellular reprogramming.
items.explanation: H3K9me3 acts as a barrier to cellular reprogramming, not as an activating mark.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 2) | EPIGENETICS OF METABOLISM
items.statement: Dietary intake of alcohol can directly contribute to acetate levels and influence histone acetylation in the brain.
items.explanation: Dietary intake of alcohol can directly contribute to acetate levels, affecting histone acetylation in the brain with effects on gene transcription involved in learning and memory.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 2) | EPIGENETICS OF METABOLISM

items.subtopic: EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 2) | EPIGENETICS OF METABOLISM
items.statement: High-fat diets increase histone acetylation by enhancing the production of acetyl-CoA.
items.explanation: High-fat diets reduce histone acetylation through their ability to inhibit enzymes that produce acetyl-CoA.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 2) | EPIGENETICS OF METABOLISM

items.statement: EZH2 is a tumor suppressor gene.
items.explanation: EZH2 is an oncogene, not a tumor suppressor.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · CANCER EPIGENETICS (part 1)

items.statement: MLL1 translocations are common in both adult and pediatric acute myeloid leukemia.
items.explanation: The passage states that MLL1 translocations occur in adult and pediatric AML.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · CANCER EPIGENETICS (part 1)

items.statement: EZH2 inhibitors are being developed for use in lymphomas, sarcomas, and melanoma.
items.explanation: Targeted EZH2 inhibitors have shown early clinical success in lymphomas, sarcomas, and melanoma.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · CANCER EPIGENETICS (part 2)

items.statement: The primary focus of epigenetic drug development is to create non-specific inhibitors.
items.explanation: The current focus is on identifying more specific inhibitors, not non-specific ones.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · CANCER EPIGENETICS (part 2)

items.statement: Loss of H3K9me3 in aged cells leads to aberrant expression of normally repressed transposable elements.
items.explanation: Aged cells display age-dependent loss of H3K9me3, leading to aberrant expression of normally repressed transposable elements.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF AGING (part 1)

items.statement: Caloric restriction decreases the expression of Sirt1 and Sirt6.
items.explanation: Caloric restriction-induced increases in Sirt1 and Sirt6 expression can delay aging.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF AGING (part 1)

items.statement: Global DNA hypomethylation occurs across the human genome with aging.
items.explanation: Aging is associated with global DNA hypomethylation.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF AGING (part 2)

items.statement: DNA methylation patterns during aging are purely stochastic and random.
items.explanation: These patterns are not merely stochastic but can predict chronologic age.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF AGING (part 2)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETICS OF THE BRAIN AND BEHAVIOR
items.statement: Prader-Willi syndrome is frequently caused by aberrant DNA methylation.
items.explanation: Prader-Willi syndrome, a neurodevelopmental condition, is often due to abnormal imprinting and aberrant DNA methylation.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF THE BRAIN AND BEHAVIOR

items.subtopic: EPIGENETICS OF THE BRAIN AND BEHAVIOR
items.statement: BDNF knockdown in the dentate gyrus leads to anxiety-like behavior in mouse models.
items.explanation: Knockdown of BDNF in the dentate gyrus leads to depression-like behavior, not anxiety-like.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF THE BRAIN AND BEHAVIOR

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 1)
items.statement: RSV infection upregulates the expression of H3K4 methyl groups on antiviral genes.
items.explanation: RSV infection promotes the expression of KDM5B, which removes H3K4 methyl groups from antiviral genes.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 1)
items.statement: Epigenetic memory can be elicited by various perturbations such as infections and vaccination.
items.explanation: Numerous perturbations including infections and vaccination have been shown to elicit an epigenetic memory.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 2) | CONCLUSION
items.statement: The chapter provides a comprehensive overview of all aspects of the chromatin and epigenetics fields.
items.explanation: The chapter offers a concise snapshot due to the enormity and complexity of the field.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 2) | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 2) | CONCLUSION
items.statement: BET inhibitors have broad effects in blocking antigen presentation and T- and B-cell activation.
items.explanation: BETi display broad effects in blocking antigen presentation and T- and B-cell activation.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 2) | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment
items.assertion: Epigenetics is one of the most innovative fields within biomedical sciences.
items.reason: The continuous introduction of powerful technologies for interrogating the epigenome has driven innovation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why epigenetics is considered innovative.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment

items.assertion: Histone acetylation and methylation are the most well-studied posttranslational modifications.
items.reason: They are the most abundant histone posttranslational modifications studied in chromatin regulation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why these modifications are well-studied.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · THE BIOCHEMICAL BASES OF EPIGENETICS (part 1)

items.assertion: TET enzymes convert 5-methylcytosine to forms that DNMT1 cannot recognize.
items.reason: This conversion allows for the passive loss or active removal of methylation, potentially returning to unmethylated cytosine.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the role of TET enzymes in demethylation.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · THE BIOCHEMICAL BASES OF EPIGENETICS (part 1)

items.assertion: H3K36me3 is involved in transcriptional elongation and splicing.
items.reason: H3K36me3 recruits other methyltransferases, impacting both DNA and RNA methylation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; however, H3K36me3's role in transcriptional elongation and splicing is not explained by its recruitment of other methyltransferases.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · THE BIOCHEMICAL BASES OF EPIGENETICS (part 2)

items.assertion: The SWI/SNF family nucleosome remodeling complex represses transcription.
items.reason: SWI/SNF creates nucleosome-free regions for transcriptional activation.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the SWI/SNF family activates transcription by creating nucleosome-free regions.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · THE BIOCHEMICAL BASES OF EPIGENETICS (part 2)

items.subtopic: TOOLS FOR THE STUDY OF EPIGENETICS
items.assertion: Hi-C is used to study three-dimensional genome organization.
items.reason: Hi-C is a high-throughput form of chromosome conformation capture technology.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · TOOLS FOR THE STUDY OF EPIGENETICS

items.subtopic: TOOLS FOR THE STUDY OF EPIGENETICS
items.assertion: Spatial technologies provide insights into tissue pathology by altering histone acetylation.
items.reason: Spatial technologies capture molecular data at subcellular resolution within native tissue context.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because spatial technologies do not alter histone acetylation; they capture molecular data. The reason is true.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · TOOLS FOR THE STUDY OF EPIGENETICS

items.assertion: Disruption of DNA methylation can lead to imprinting disorders.
items.reason: DNA methylation alterations are involved in coordinating expression of imprinted genes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 1)

items.assertion: Excessive self-renewal of adult stem cells promotes cancer.
items.reason: Impaired self-renewal can lead to stem cell exhaustion and premature aging.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 2) | EPIGENETICS OF METABOLISM
items.assertion: Loss of epigenetic regulation can lead to diseases associated with aging.
items.reason: Epigenetic modifiers shape cell-type specificity and influence differentiation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not directly explain why loss of epigenetic regulation leads to diseases associated with aging.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 2) | EPIGENETICS OF METABOLISM

items.subtopic: EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 2) | EPIGENETICS OF METABOLISM
items.assertion: Dietary methionine availability affects histone H3K4me3 levels.
items.reason: SAM is a key cofactor for histone and DNA methylation, influenced by dietary factors.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains how dietary methionine availability affects histone H3K4me3 levels through SAM.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION (part 2) | EPIGENETICS OF METABOLISM

items.assertion: Histone H3.3 mutations can lead to global defects in histone modifications.
items.reason: These mutations, such as K27M and K36M, result in dominant negative effects that inhibit the cognate enzymes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · CANCER EPIGENETICS (part 1)

items.assertion: Epigenetic mechanisms only contribute to cancer by activating oncogenes.
items.reason: This is not entirely accurate as epigenetics also affects chemotherapy resistance and antitumor immunity.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because epigenetic mechanisms have broader roles beyond just activating oncogenes.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · CANCER EPIGENETICS (part 1)

items.assertion: Exercise reduces activity of HDACs 4 and 5.
items.reason: This leads to increased H3K36ac in skeletal muscle, promoting beneficial gene expression.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF AGING (part 1)

items.assertion: Loss of Sirt6 results in extended lifespan.
items.reason: Sirtuin deacetylases promote health span and lifespan across species.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; loss of Sirt6 results in premature aging, not extended lifespan.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF AGING (part 1)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETICS OF THE BRAIN AND BEHAVIOR
items.assertion: REST levels increase with aging and serve a protective function in neurons.
items.reason: This is due to its ability to recruit chromatin regulatory enzymes, such as histone deacetylases and methyltransferases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF THE BRAIN AND BEHAVIOR

items.subtopic: EPIGENETICS OF THE BRAIN AND BEHAVIOR
items.assertion: Opioid exposure leads to reduced histone acetylation.
items.reason: This promotes a more open and permissive state of chromatin, reinforcing reward-seeking behaviors.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; opioid exposure actually increases histone acetylation.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETICS OF THE BRAIN AND BEHAVIOR

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 1)
items.assertion: Influenza virus infection leads to the upregulation of H3K9me3 methyltransferase SETDB2.
items.reason: This upregulation blocks expression of CXCL1 and NF-κB target genes involved in neutrophil attraction.
items.explanation: Both assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 1)
items.assertion: Differential DNA methylation at the ACE2 gene may impact SARS-CoV-2 infection.
items.reason: This differential methylation alters expression levels of ACE2, affecting viral entry into host cells.
items.explanation: Both assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 2) | CONCLUSION
items.assertion: The coming years will bring important additions to the field of epigenetics.
items.reason: There is exponential growth in uncovering the interface between the epigenome and epigenetic therapies with the environment and disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Role of Epigenetics in Disease and Treatment · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON INFECTION, IMMUNITY, AND INFLAMMATION (part 2) | CONCLUSION
